alternatives_considered: Apply database permissions change with feature file regeneration validation
Apply database permissions change without downstream impact assessment
Stage database change in canary environment first
constraints_operative: Database permissions changes classified as routine infrastructure operations
No cross-boundary impact assessment required for metadata-only changes
Feature file generation pipeline operates independently of database governance
decision_context._recoverability_note: Partially recoverable: inputs and constraints were documented in operational records, but alternatives_considered is reconstructed post-hoc from the engineering blog rather than captured at decision time. The evaluative context (why cross-boundary impact was not assessed) was not recorded.

decision_logic.rule_version: proxy-wasm-feature-loader v3.2 with 200-feature hard-coded preallocation limit
decision_logic._recoverability_note: Recoverable: the hard-coded 200-feature limit, Rust panic behavior, and global propagation mechanism were fully documented in the post-incident engineering blog and internal root cause analysis.

decision_boundary._recoverability_note: Recoverable: the cross-subsystem cascade path (database -> metadata pipeline -> feature generator -> proxy fleet) was fully reconstructed post-incident. However, no single monitoring scope covered this interaction path at decision time.

uncertainty_flags: Database permissions change not assessed for downstream data shape impact
Duplicate column entries in metadata query not detected by validation
Feature file size increase exceeded hard-coded memory limit without graceful handling
Intermittent failure pattern (good/bad config alternation across shards) mimicked DDoS signature
decision_quality_indicators._recoverability_note: Structurally unfillable: uncertainty flags listed here were identified post-hoc. At decision time, no mechanism existed to flag that a database permissions change could propagate through the metadata pipeline to crash the proxy fleet. The hard-coded 200-feature limit had no corresponding quality indicator.

human_override_record.override_decision: Incident initially classified as DDoS attack; root cause identified as infrastructure change only after extended investigation
human_override_record.independence_assessment: Operations team relied on monitoring dashboards designed for known failure modes; cross-boundary failure was not a recognized category
human_override_record.basis_for_deviation: Failure pattern matched external attack signature more closely than internal configuration error
human_override_record._recoverability_note: Epistemically void: human override records exist but document response to symptoms rather than governance of the causal decision. The operations team could not override the database permissions change because the causal chain was invisible; their 'override' was incident response to a misclassified symptom.

temporal_metadata._recoverability_note: Recoverable: deployment timestamps and incident timeline were documented in the engineering blog. Ground truth (root cause identification) arrived same day, approximately 3 hours after the initial database change.
